proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam description provides strong gameplay evidence: the game explicitly centers on turn-based battles, leveling up, gaining experience, growing bonds to unlock new abilities, traveling with a friend party, and using pair-specific Link Skills in combat. It also emphasizes an authored story about saving the world, explorable locations across two connected worlds, and environmental puzzles tied to world-switching. That combination strongly fits a story-forward JRPG structure with party progression and turn-based tactical encounters. Secondary CRPG/tactical signals exist from party management and puzzle-solving, but the strongest identity is JRPG-style story RPG rather than pure tactics or point-and-click adventure.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on party-based turn-based RPG structure, companion ability synergy, authored adventure, and regular environmental puzzle solving between battles. Less aligned on modern-world mystery and bond-social progression specifics.

Strong fit on story-first indie RPG identity, turn-based party combat, emotional bonds, and dual-world exploration. Less clear on puzzle-solving density and mechanical party-link customization than the anchor.

Shares mystery-driven campaign, close friend group, relationship progression feeding combat, and movement between ordinary and alternate spaces. However, its calendar/social-sim dungeon loop is structurally more distinct than the anchor's exploration-puzzle JRPG flow.

Important comparison point for modern-setting, eccentric party JRPGs with linear exploration and turn-based progression. But available evidence here is thin, and it lacks the anchor's explicit puzzle/world-switching and bond-based combat emphasis.
Compact story RPG with a teen ensemble, turn-based party combat, relationship-driven character skills, and heavy overworld puzzle solving.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present an action-RPG loop centered on choosing one of four hero classes, fighting hordes of enemies and dragon bosses, acquiring skill loot, optimizing builds through the Ancestral Grid, evolving character and gear, and continuing into endgame activities. The game also emphasizes up-to-4-player co-op across the full adventure and a hub-city upgrade layer, which fits a co-op-capable loot-driven action RPG more than any match-based or shooter taxonomy result suggested by the noisy computed near-misses. Evidence is strong for dominant combat, class/build differentiation, gear/skill progression, and replayable campaign structure, but insufficient to lock perspective or exact world topology more precisely.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Class-based isometric action RPG with co-op play, large enemy packs, loot chasing, and endgame-focused build progression.
Four-player hack-and-slash RPG centered on distinct heroes, crowd-clearing combat, loot upgrades, and campaign-to-endgame co-op.
Fast-paced co-op dungeon-running with hordes of enemies, strong class identity, gear progression, and replayable endgame loops.
Action RPG with heavy build customization, skill specialization, loot optimization, and long-term character progression.
Accessible co-op loot ARPG with class-based combat, swarms of enemies, and continuous gear-and-skill build development.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as an 'action-packed, first-person retro shooter' set in the Starship Troopers universe, with the player joining the Mobile Infantry against the Arachnids. The Steam detailed copy adds key structure: a solo campaign 'packed with missions, secrets,' a large weapon arsenal, and 'epic battlefields,' which supports a campaign-driven first-person shooter identity rather than a match-based arena FPS. The Mobile Infantry framing, battlefield combat, and anti-Bug arsenal support a military-FPS classification despite the sci-fi setting.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Modern retro FPS with chunky sci-fi gunplay, linear campaign stages, secrets, and large hordes of enemies.
Fast throwback shooter built around aggressive movement, heavy weapons, gore, and campaign maps packed with hidden areas.
Classic-style first-person shooter focused on level exploration, key-hunting, secrets, and a solo campaign structure.
Arcade-style FPS centered on mowing down huge swarms with oversized weapons through straightforward combat-heavy missions.
Foundational retro FPS comp for speed, maze-like levels, secret discovery, and weapon-driven demon-slaying campaign flow.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a comedy adventure built around 'tactical frog battles' where players 'collect, buy and trade frogs' to 'build an unbeatable roster' while embarking on quests and exploring an absurd world. That strongly supports a creature-collection RPG identity with tactical party-based battles, with tactical_rpg as a secondary read. Perspective, exact battle format, and setting are not clearly stated, so those fields remain conservative.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Creature-collecting RPG with party-building, overworld exploration, side quests, and turn-based tactical team battles.
Open-ended monster-collecting RPG loop centered on catching creatures, building a roster, and battling through a story adventure.
Indie monster-taming RPG focused on collecting creatures, party progression, and strategic turn-based battles across an adventure campaign.
Team-building creature battler with strong party composition focus, collectible monsters, and tactical combat-driven progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Maestro as a VR orchestra conducting rhythm game. The core loop is timing- and gesture-driven: players use hand tracking to keep tempo, cue instruments, and shape music in real time, which strongly supports rhythm_game over weaker near-miss candidates. The game is described as playing through tracks/concert performances rather than exploration or narrative progression, fitting match_session structure, timing_input, rhythm_timing mechanics, and the hit_beats goal. It is single-player, non-combat, and presented as a premium replayable track-based experience with unlockable stages and cosmetics. While Steam also tags it as simulation/casual, that reads as secondary framing around realistic conducting technique rather than a different core genre.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Flagship VR rhythm comp: motion-controlled, song-based, precision-timing gameplay with strong replay and mastery overlap, though its slash-and-dodge loop is more arcade-reactive than conducting-focused.

Very solid VR rhythm neighbor built on full-arm movement, timing accuracy, and immersive musical flow; closer on expressive body performance, but still more note-lane driven than Maestro’s orchestral direction.

VR instrument-performance rhythm game with physical drumming motions, track progression, and mastery. Good overlap in musical embodiment, though its arcade campaign and demon-smashing framing push it away from Maestro’s refined conducting sim.

Another VR rhythm game with gesture-based strikes and track-by-track score chasing. Strong on tempo play and session structure, but the drumming/racing hybrid is less aligned with Maestro’s expressive hand-conducting identity.
VR music performance/creation with hand-led expressive control and stage presence, closer to conducting feel than target-hitting rhythm games.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports an online shared-world sandbox adventure with third-person exploration, building, terrain destruction, co-op/MMO-style social play, and some monster fighting. However, the core gameplay identity does not cleanly match any allowed archetype. It is not well-supported as mmo_action_rpg because progression structure, RPG build identity, and combat loop are underspecified; not open_world_action_adventure because construction/sandbox settlement play appears equally or more central than authored action-adventure structure; and not exploration_survival_adventure because survival pressures are absent. The current mapping-gap signals also show no candidate archetype reached threshold. Because the evidence is concrete but points to a gameplay blend outside the available taxonomy rather than a strong fit within it, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a mismatched archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on colorful voxel presentation, online/shared-world play, building, exploration, destructibility, and light action combat; heavier class/loot focus than Altiros, but still the clearest benchmark.

Persistent online voxel sandbox with settlement building, world sculpting, exploration, and social progression maps well to Altiros's MMO-like sandbox identity; less explicitly action-combat-driven and more economy/city oriented.

Strong fit on third-person co-op sandbox adventuring, block building, island-based exploration, and real-time combat. It is more session-based and RPG-structured than Altiros's shared-world sandbox emphasis.

Good comp for building, terrain manipulation, exploration, creatures, and multiplayer sandbox play, but it leans more into survival/crafting systems and less into the cute action-adventure MMO social identity Altiros presents.
Shared-world block sandbox with freeform building, terrain destruction, exploration, and light combat as parallel pillars.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Pizza Slice as a restaurant business simulation centered on managing a family pizzeria: shopping for ingredients, prep work, making pizzas, expanding tables, buying better kitchenware, learning recipes, keeping customers happy, and growing from a small shop into a larger franchise. Metacritic explicitly calls it a strategy/simulation game with economic elements and a pizzeria manager role. Steam categories also support single-player, co-op, and PvP, with the detailed description naming an additive team-vs-team cook-off mode; however, the dominant identity remains the core business-management sandbox rather than competitive match play. The evidence strongly fits management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue and franchise growth, not urban planning or autonomous colony management.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct pizzeria economic sim with chain expansion, recipe creation, competition, and rise-from-small-shop progression; this is the clearest gameplay-identity match.
Very close on pizza-order fulfillment, hands-on making, upgrades, rival pressure, and keeping the shop afloat, though typically lighter in management scope.
Strong restaurant-ownership sandbox with menu design, layout, staff, recipes, and business growth; less specifically pizza-focused but highly aligned on core tycoon loop.
Hospitality tycoon with supply chain, staff management, customer satisfaction, recipe creation, and business expansion, but café management is a looser fit than restaurant/pizzeria sims.
Blends hands-on cooking with restaurant layout, upgrades, automation, and prominent co-op play.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Mythmatch as a narrative-rich town/community-building merge game focused on rebuilding Ithaca, matching items and people, relationships, and replayable god-themed minigames. However, the evidence does not strongly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a city_builder, farming_sim, life_sim, management_tycoon, or puzzle-led archetype within the available list, and the referenced minigames appear additive rather than the core identity. Provider metadata also produces weak or noisy signals (for example co-op/multiplayer categories without concrete gameplay structure, and a simulation genre tag). Because the game’s core gameplay identity appears to sit outside the current archetype set, classification into an existing archetype would be speculative.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strong overlap in cozy narrative progression, helping a cast of characters, and building/improving a shared home space, but Spiritfarer is fundamentally a management/exploration/crafting game rather than a merge-driven town builder.
Community rebuilding and town development are central, with varied sub-activities supporting a larger restoration loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a short first-person 'walking simulator with extra mechanics' focused on atmosphere, light fishing, and hide-and-seek/chase sequences with a revenge-seeking beaver. The horror framing is present, but there is not enough evidence for a supported core archetype such as survival_horror, psychological_horror, action_horror, or stealth_action_adventure: combat is not established, resource-survival structure is absent, puzzle structure is unclear, and stealth is only implied by hide-and-seek/chase wording. The available text points more strongly to a short first-person narrative/walking-sim experience, which is not an available taxonomy archetype here, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: First-person woodland horror focused on exploration, light puzzle interaction, and being pursued rather than fighting.
A first-person narrative thriller with walking-sim structure, environmental storytelling, and sustained tension over action.
First-person exploration mystery with a strong atmospheric focus and minimal mechanics beyond investigation and traversal.
A compact first-person narrative exploration game with a walking-sim core and strong sense of place, though less horror-driven.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify the game as a 'mini metroidvania.' The gameplay copy supports that label with side-scrolling platforming, alternative paths, exploration across 4 biomes, dangerous creatures, powerful ancient items, boss fights, and a simplified experience system. Visual presentation is clearly 2D pixel art, and the game is single-player with a compact but authored campaign structure. While some combat specifics are not deeply detailed, the core exploration-gating-plus-bosses structure is strong enough to classify it as metroidvania.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: a short, streamlined 2D metroidvania with exploration, secrets, power-up gating, bosses, and a concise campaign structure similar to the anchor's mini scope.
Another compact metroidvania built around traversal/item unlocks, handcrafted map exploration, and boss encounters; slightly more combat/mech-ability defined, but still a strong audience match.

Colorful, approachable exploration-platformer with interconnected areas, multiple forms unlocking traversal options, and adjustable difficulty; strong structural overlap though it is more of a classic action-adventure/metroidvania hybrid.
Clear metroidvania overlap in side-scrolling exploration, ability gating, secrets, and bosses, but it is more combat- and reflex-driven than the anchor's lightweight, casual mini-metroidvania feel.
Compact, friendly metroidvania with light combat, smooth traversal, upgrade-gated exploration, and a breezy overall challenge level.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently support a short, single-player, third-person, story-driven exploration adventure with light logic puzzles, item use, collectibles, and humor in a sci-fi facility setting. However, that evidence does not strongly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is explicitly not puzzle-heavy, does not show clear platforming emphasis, does not establish meaningful combat loops, and lacks the stronger authored action/setpiece profile needed for cinematic_action_adventure or the horror/combat grounding needed for action_horror, survival_horror, or psychological_horror. This appears closer to a light narrative exploration adventure/walking-adventure hybrid, which is not an available archetype in the allowed list.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on compact sci-fi facility exploration, environmental storytelling, low mechanical complexity, and story-first progression; lighter on puzzles and lacks the anchor's third-person presentation, but the core loop is highly compatible.

Shared trapped-in-facility narrative, guided exploration, item/puzzle-driven progression, and moderate runtime fit well, but Bradwell leans more puzzle-centric and first-person than the anchor's lighter, more relaxed structure.

Strong match for short, self-contained, story-led exploration with simple interactions and discovery-driven pacing, though its anthology structure, first-person perspective, and very different tone/world model make it a looser fit than the sci-fi facility comps.
Third-person sci-fi narrative adventure with exploration-first pacing, light puzzles, and a concise authored campaign.
Third-person sci-fi exploration with light puzzle solving, simple tool-based interaction, and a broadly accessible challenge level.

title: Monster Hunter Stories 3: Twisted Reflection

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently frames this as an RPG in the Monster Hunter Stories series where the player 'become[s] a Rider,' 'rais[es] and bond[s] with your favorite monsters,' and 'explore[s] the vibrant world of Monster Hunter.' Those are strong identity signals for a monster-collect RPG, especially the explicit monster bonding/raising loop. The narrative copy also emphasizes a story-led journey across countries with a Rider and trusted Rathalos, supporting a campaign-driven heroic adventure structure. Evidence for exact perspective, combat format, and more detailed structural facets is limited, so those fields are left blank rather than inferred too aggressively.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Monster Hunter Stories 2: Wings of Ruin
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Monster Hunter Stories 2: Wings of Ruin
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor with the same Rider/Monstie loop, turn-based battles, monster hatching, and story-led progression.
The original template for the series’ monster-collecting, turn-based RPG structure and Rider-focused exploration.
A party-based turn-based RPG built around recruiting, fusing, and strategically using collectible creatures in battle.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Dragon Quest Monsters: The Dark Prince
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Dragon Quest Monsters: The Dark Prince
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Monster-taming JRPG with turn-based combat, team building, scouting, and progression driven by raising creatures.
Creature-collecting, turn-based RPG focused on capturing monsters, party composition, and a lighter storybook adventure structure.

missing_must_include_titles: Monster Hunter Stories 2: Wings of Ruin

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies the game as a baseball simulation: genre labels include simulation and sports, theme label is baseball, and the description emphasizes 'true-to-life on-field action' plus core sports-sim mode pillars like Road To The Show, Franchise, and Diamond Dynasty. That supports a sports_sim classification with baseball theming and both long-form career/franchise play and match-based play. Some fine-grained fingerprint details like camera perspective are not explicitly stated in the stored text, so confidence is strong but not maximal.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Immediate predecessor with essentially the same realistic baseball sim identity, on-field play, and core mode suite.
Another near-identical annual MLB The Show entry with the same direct-action baseball simulation and career/franchise structure.
Still extremely close in core batting, pitching, fielding, and mode design, just slightly further removed than the latest predecessors.
Strong baseball game overlap with hands-on play and franchise modes, but its arcade-inspired feel is meaningfully looser than The Show’s sim-first identity.
Same-series MLB sim with the same direct on-field gameplay and comparable Road To The Show, Franchise, and Diamond Dynasty structure.

title: Eve of the 12 Months

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence directly identifies the game as a 'dramatic visual novel' in both description and OpenCritic text, and Metacritic labels its genre as Visual Novel. The premise centers on a character-driven Christmas Eve encounter and unfolding story, with no evidence of combat or action systems. That strongly supports a visual_novel classification with linear, single-player, non-combat, dialogue-driven storytelling.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: A short, linear visual novel emphasizing atmosphere, character drama, and a fully authored reading experience over branching systems.

unresolved_similar_games: planetarian: The Reverie of a Little Planet

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a soccer-focused game built around discrete matches: 'casual street soccer,' 3v3 and 4v4 play, and a 'Competitive Cup Mode' with tournament-style gameplay. The strongest supported identity signals are soccer as the sports theme, match-based session structure, and the goal of winning matches. Custom courts, ball designs, and floor styles read as cosmetic/environmental variation rather than a different genre. While perspective, presentation, and exact mode details are not specified, the gameplay loop is concrete enough to support sports_sim.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Arcade soccer built around small-team matches, exaggerated play, and quick competitive sessions.
Modern street-soccer game centered on stylized courts, arcade controls, and alternate small-team modes.
Simple, fast-paced arcade football with short match loops and local multiplayer-friendly structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a semi-open world puzzle adventure centered on combining magical runes to create spells, exploring interconnected areas, solving environmental puzzles with multiple solutions, and pursuing a branching mystery narrative. That evidence is strong for a systemic puzzle-adventure identity, but the available taxonomy archetypes do not cleanly cover this profile. The strongest supported traits are semi-open exploration, environmental puzzle solving, authored branching narrative, and systemic spell-based problem solving; combat is not described as a primary loop, and the evidence does not firmly establish a supported archetype such as open_world_action_adventure, western_narrative_rpg, or immersive_sim_action. Because the game's core gameplay identity falls between taxonomy options and forcing a combat- or RPG-led archetype would misclassify it, rejection is more appropriate than a weak accept.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on exploration-led puzzle play, versatile ability combinations, gated world discovery, and a large interconnected environment; weaker on narrative/branching choices.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent match for open-ended traversal, systemic ability use, and multiple-solution environmental problem-solving, though it is more combat-driven and broader in open-world action scope.

Strong overlap in mystery-through-exploration, world-as-puzzle structure, and uncovering how systems and locations connect, but lacks the anchor's spell-combination sandbox and branching character-driven questing.

Good fit for narrative mystery, exploration, dialogue choices, side-character involvement, and multiple endings, but it is more deduction/dialogue-led than systemic multi-solution puzzle experimentation.
Spell crafting directly drives exploration and environmental puzzle solving, with adventure structure closer to the anchor than combat-led action games.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Parkour Labs as a movement-centric 3D parkour/platforming game built around speed, flow, precision, chaining techniques, discovering shortcuts, and unlocking new challenges across stylized surreal environments. The gameplay identity is strongly non-combat and mastery-driven, which aligns better with precision_platformer than action_platformer. Level/route unlocking and secret discovery provide a lighter secondary resemblance to 3d_collectathon, but the core loop is skillful traversal rather than collection. Multiplayer/PvP is supported by store metadata, though the main descriptive copy emphasizes single-player challenge runs and mastery. VR support introduces a first-person play option, while the broader labeling and non-VR presentation support a third-person 3D platformer identity overall.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on fast parkour/platforming, momentum mastery, level-based challenge runs, shortcuts, and speed focus. Combat tools are more prominent than in the anchor, but traversal still drives the core loop.

Excellent fit on first-person speed-platforming, replayable stages, aggressive momentum, and time-improvement challenge structure. More hardcore and arena-like than the anchor, but the traversal mastery loop is highly similar.

Good non-combat movement comp with jumping, sliding, climbing, and environmental traversal. However, its peaceful open-ended tone and low-pressure structure are less aligned with the anchor's skill-test and shortcut-optimization emphasis.
VR-native precision climbing and freerunning focused on flow, speed, and skill mastery.
Traversal-led first-person platforming with momentum, fluid movement, and minimal combat emphasis.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a single-player open-world space combat simulator with spaceship dogfights, trading, mining, mission-taking, ship customization, and exploration across star systems. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a space-combat-sim / open-galaxy trader-pilot archetype, and the title does not cleanly fit any available accepted archetype better than a forced mismatch. Rejecting because the gameplay identity is strong but outside the supported archetype set.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Single-player space sim focused on bounty hunting, trading, mission-running, cockpit combat, and ship customization.
Shares space combat, exploration, quests, loot and ship progression, though it is more action-RPG and less trade-sim oriented.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a fast-paced 'survivor-style action roguelite' built around quick replayable runs, endless monster waves, hero unlocks, random power-ups, and build synergies in fantasy arenas. Metacritic also labels it a third-person shooter, but the descriptive copy emphasizes a broader survivor/bullet-heaven style action loop rather than any supported taxonomy archetype here. The closest allowed archetype would be co_op_action_roguelite, but there is no evidence of co-op. Shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or third_person_cover_shooter are not well supported by the actual loop description. Because the game's apparent core identity does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype and forcing a fit would be misleading, rejection is appropriate.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Definitive wave-survival roguelite built on short runs, escalating hordes, meta-unlocks, and synergistic power-up builds.
Arena-based survivor action with hero selection, fantasy attacks, rapid build crafting, and heavier active combat spectacle.
Fantasy horde-survival runs focused on surviving waves, collecting loot and upgrades, and building stronger characters over repeated attempts.
Another highly replayable survivors-like centered on quick sessions, character variety, run-based upgrades, and surviving escalating waves.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text gives concrete gameplay, not just genre tags: it explicitly calls Anarchy Road a 'Post-Apocalyptic action driving game' where four heroes 'race against the clock,' 'battle bosses,' 'blast enemies,' and 'dodge traps.' Combined with racing/sports labels and co-op/split-screen categories, this supports an arcade-racer identity with strong vehicular combat and boss-focused setpiece encounters rather than a realistic racer or party game.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on arcade combat-racing loop: weapons, obstacles, fast rounds, and strong local/online multiplayer. Slightly less aligned because it is more pure competitive survival racing than boss-focused action driving.

Excellent match for chaotic same-screen arcade vehicle combat with weapon powerups, quick respawns, party structure, and solo support. A bit more party-brawler and less race-against-the-clock/PvE than the anchor.

Strong fit on high-speed arcade combat racing with aggressive weapons and destructive race flow. It is more hardcore competitive track racing and less couch-co-op/party-forward or boss-encounter-driven than the anchor appears to be.
Run-based vehicular combat with checkpoint racing, enemy takedowns, boss encounters, and a stronger PvE progression feel.
Arcade car combat racer with weapons, aggressive obstacle-heavy tracks, and a stronger action-driving identity than party racers.
